definition: A type of insurance that provides financial protection to the beneficiaries of the policyholder in case of the policyholder’s death
A type of insurance that provides coverage for medical expenses and costs associated with illness or injury
A statistical principle that states that as the number of events increases, the actual results will be closer to the expected results
A financial product that provides a series of payments to the annuitant (usually at retirement) in exchange for a lump sum payment
The potential of losing something of value
Risks that have the potential for loss or gain
Risks that have the potential for loss only
An event or circumstance that may cause loss or damage
A condition that increases the likelihood or severity of loss or damage
The risk that an insured party may act recklessly or dishonestly because they are protected by insurance
The risk that an insured party may act negligently or carelessly because they are protected by insurance
A method of handling risk by avoiding the risk altogether
A method of handling risk by transferring the risk to another party, typically through insurance
A method of handling risk by accepting the risk and bearing the financial consequences if a loss occurs
A method of handling risk by taking steps to reduce the likelihood or severity of the risk
A risk that meets the criteria for insurance coverage, including being measurable, having a potential for loss, and not being catastrophic
The economic value of an individual’s life is based on their income, potential earnings, and other factors
The tendency of individuals with a higher risk of loss to seek insurance coverage more than those with a lower risk, leading to higher premiums
The practice of spreading risk across a larger group of individuals or entities to reduce the impact of loss on any single member
Techniques and strategies used to manage and mitigate potential risks, including avoidance, transfer, retention, and reduction
A licensed individual who legally represents the insurer in insurance transactions.
A licensed individual who legally represents the applicant and insured, shopping among insurers on their behalf.
An insurer incorporated under the laws of the state in which it is selling (e.g., a Florida-incorporated insurer selling in Florida).
An insurer incorporated in another U.S. state from the viewpoint of the state where it sells.
An insurer incorporated in another country from the viewpoint of the U.S. state where it sells.
A specialized insurer that accepts portions of risk transferred from other insurance companies.
National Association of Insurance Commissioners — state regulators who promote uniformity through model laws; it has no enforcement authority.
The 1945 federal law establishing that the states, not the federal government, are the primary regulators of insurance.
State entity that pays covered claims, within limits, when a member insurer becomes insolvent.
The law giving the state power to fine, issue cease-and-desist orders, and impose penalties for prohibited practices.
Giving anything of value as an inducement to buy insurance; legal in Florida only under the strict conditions of §626.572.
Disclosure document that helps applicants understand and compare policies; it does not guarantee the lowest price.
Rules governing testimonials, statistics, and special offers in insurance marketing.
A liability insurer formed by similar businesses or professionals to insure their own risks as a group.
Marketing system that sells policies by mail, phone, and internet without agents or brokers.
Home service insurance with small face amounts and premiums collected frequently, often weekly, at the home.
Not an insurer — a marketplace where member syndicates underwrite risks.
Federal law passed after the September 11th attacks imposing anti-money-laundering obligations that affect insurers.
The authority an insurer grants a licensee to transact business on its behalf; a license terminates after four years without one.
The state license that makes an insurance company competent and authorized to transact insurance.
In reinsurance, the insurer that transfers (cedes) risk to the reinsurer. Reinsurance is strictly company-to-company.
Nonprofit insurer with a lodge system, ritualistic work, and elected officers; issues certificates (e.g., Knights of Columbus).
A mutual insurer (owned by policyholders) converting into a stock company (owned by stockholders).
SPECIFIC disclosure: cost indexes and figures for the exact policy proposed (contrast the buyer's guide = generic education).
A feature of insurance contracts that there is an element of chance for both parties and that the dollar given by the policyholder (premiums) and the insurer (benefits) may not be equal
One element of a binding contract; the company’s acceptance of premium payment and statements made by the prospective insured and the application
In contract law, the requirement that the object of, or reason for, the contract must be legal.
A life insurance policy is a contract of adhesion because buyers must adhere to the terms of the contract already in existence. They have no opportunity to negotiate terms, rates, values, and so on.
Distinguishing characteristic of an insurance contract and that it is only the insurance company that pledges anything.
Signed transfer of benefits of a policy by an insured to another party. The company does not guarantee the validity of an assignment.
A contract of insurance that pays a stated amount in the event of a loss
Failure of the insured to disclose to the company a fact material to the acceptance of the risk at the time application is made

Statements made by applicants on their applications for insurance that they represent as being substantially true to the best of their knowledge and belief, but that are not warranted as exact in every detail

Statements made on an application for insurance that are warranted to be true. That is, they are exact in every detail as opposed to representations. Statements on applications for insurance are rarely warranties, unless fraud is involved.
The requirement of insurance contracts that loss must be sustained by the applicant upon the death or disability of another, and loss must be sufficient to warrant compensation.

Life insurance arrangements where investors persuade consumers to take out new life insurance policies, with the investors named as beneficiaries. Investors loan money to the insured to pay the premiums, and the insured ultimately assigns ownership of the policy to the investors who receive the death benefits when the insured dies. Because the investors are the constructive applicants, owners, and beneficiaries of the policies, and they have no insurable interest in the insured, many states view STOLI arrangements as fraudulent.
The specific authority given in writing to the agent in the contract of the agency.
Authority not specifically granted to the agent in the contract of agency, but which common sense dictates the agent has. It enables the agent to carry out routine responsibilities.
The authority an agent appears to have based on the principal’s (the insurer’s) actions, words, deeds, or because of circumstances the principal (the insurer) created.

term: Error and omissions professional liability insurance
definition: Professional liability insurance that protects an insurance producer against claims arising from service the producer rendered or failed to render.
Legal impediment to denying the consequences of one’s actions or deeds if they lead to detrimental actions by another.
Rule of contract law that brings all verbal statements into their written contract and disallows any changes or modifications to the contract by oral evidence.
A contract that can be made void at the option of one or more parties to the agreement.
An agreement without legal effect: An invalid contract.
Professional liability coverage protecting the AGENT against groundless, false, or fraudulent lawsuits over errors or omissions.

Legally able to contract: sane, sober, of legal age (FL: minors 15+ may contract for insurance). A person under the influence = NOT competent. An insurer is competent via its Certificate of Authority.

A receipt given to an applicant for insurance when they submit their initial premium payment with their application. It provides temporary coverage for the applicant until the policy is issued or declined.

An insurance company evaluates an applicant’s risk and decides whether to issue a policy and at what premium rate. The process involves gathering information about the applicant’s age, health, occupation, hobbies, and other factors that could affect their risk profile.
The requirement for an applicant to provide complete and accurate information about their medical history and other relevant information during the underwriting process
A method of comparing the cost of two or more life insurance policies that takes into account only the premiums paid and the death benefit received

A method of comparing the cost of two or more life insurance policies that takes into account not only the premiums paid and the death benefit received, but also the time value of money and the interest earned on the cash value component of the policy.

The process of replacing an existing life insurance policy with a new policy. Replacement is subject to regulatory oversight and requires a comparison of the costs and benefits of the existing and proposed policies.
The illegal practice of persuading an applicant to surrender an existing policy and replace it with a new policy that offers no significant advantage to the applicant
The illegal practice of repeatedly replacing a policy for the sole purpose of generating commissions for the agent, without regard for the best interests of the policyholder

A non-profit organization that maintains a database of medical information about insurance applicants. Insurance companies can use the MIB to access an applicant’s medical history and other relevant information during the underwriting process.
A report that provides information about an applicant’s home or business property. Insurance companies may use an inspection report to assess the risk of insuring the property.
Front-page clause signed by the insurer's president and secretary — the company's basic promise to pay covered benefits.
Considered: physical condition, medical history, occupation, personal habits/moral hazards, even credit rating. NOT considered: marital status. (Interest rates = premium math, not a risk factor.)
A type of insurance that covers the cost of medical and surgical expenses for individuals or groups
A type of health insurance that covers the cost of medical expenses, including hospitalization, surgery, and diagnostic tests
A type of insurance that provides financial protection for individuals who become disabled and cannot work
A type of insurance that pays out a benefit if the insured dies or is injured because of an accident
A type of health insurance that an individual purchases to cover their own medical expenses
A type of health insurance that is provided by an employer or other organization for a group of people
Programs that provide health insurance or financial assistance for healthcare costs, such as Medicaid and Medicare
A federal health insurance program for individuals who are 65 or older, as well as individuals with certain disabilities or illnesses
A joint federal and state program that provides healthcare coverage for individuals with limited income and resources
Provisions in the Old-Age, Survivors, and Disability Insurance program that provide financial assistance for individuals with disabilities
A method of calculating insurance premiums based on the experience of the insured group, which can result in dividends being paid to policyholders
The costs associated with administering an insurance policy and paying out claims
Funds set aside by an insurer to cover future claims and to ensure that they have the financial resources to pay out claims
Requests made by policyholders for payment from an insurance policy
An insurance policy that reimburses the policyholder for covered expenses
An insurance policy that pays out a predetermined amount in the event of a covered loss
An insurance policy that reimburses the policyholder for the actual cost of a covered loss
The rate of illness or disease in a particular population
Claims experience, age & sex of the insured, and benefit amounts. ('Salary benefits' is the planted wrong answer.)
Insurance companies that provide coverage to businesses and individuals for a fee
An insurance plan that reimburses policyholders for covered medical expenses after they have been incurred
The ability of a policyholder to assign their insurance benefits to a healthcare provider
A type of managed care plan that allows members to choose between in-network and out-of-network providers
A type of managed care plan that allows members to choose from a network of preferred providers or seek care from out-of-network providers at a higher cost
A federal health insurance program for individuals aged 65 and over, as well as for those with certain disabilities
This insurance provides income replacement to individuals who are unable to work due to illness or injury.
A state-run insurance program that provides benefits to workers who are injured or become ill as a result of their job
Benefits provided to individuals who have a disability, including healthcare coverage, income replacement, and other forms of assistance
State laws that require employers to provide workers’ compensation insurance to employees who are injured or become ill as a result of their job
A method of funding insurance coverage by setting aside funds to cover potential losses instead of paying premiums to an insurance company
An arrangement in which an insurer provides administrative services, such as claims processing, to a self-insured employer
An arrangement in which an independent organization provides administrative services, such as claims processing, to an insurance company or self-insured employer
An insurance plan that allows policyholders to pay a minimum premium with the possibility of additional charges based on claims experience
An arrangement in which multiple small employers join together to provide insurance coverage to their employees
An arrangement in which multiple employers join together to provide welfare benefits, such as healthcare coverage, to their employees
Healthcare providers, such as doctors, hospitals, and clinics, that provide medical services to patients
A type of managed care plan that provides healthcare services to members for a fixed fee
HMO payment method: physicians are prepaid a fixed amount per member ('per head') regardless of services used.
Government health program for active-duty military personnel and their families.
First-dollar coverage (little or no deductible) but low limits and narrow scope.
Broad catastrophic coverage with high maximums, deductibles, and coinsurance.
The amount the insured pays before the plan pays anything.
One deductible applies when several family members are injured in the same accident.
Expenses from the last 3 months of the year count toward next year's deductible.
After the deductible, the plan and insured share costs at a set ratio, commonly 80/20.
The cap on the insured's out-of-pocket costs; beyond it the plan pays 100%.
Plans pay what area providers typically charge; amounts above UCR are the insured's responsibility.
Individually owned, triple tax-advantaged account; requires a high-deductible health plan; funds roll over and are portable.
Employer-sponsored account funded pre-tax by the employee; use-it-or-lose-it.
Employer-funded only; reimburses medical expenses; rollover at the employer's discretion.
A plan with a high deductible and capped out-of-pocket costs; required for HSA eligibility.
Insurance that covers the cost of medical expenses incurred by the insured, such as doctor visits and hospital stays
A level of health insurance coverage defined by the Affordable Care Act, which includes essential health benefits such as hospitalization, prescription drugs, and maternity care
The process of being paid back for expenses incurred, typically by submitting a claim to an insurance company
Costs that have been accrued or accumulated, typically in relation to medical treatment or procedures
Insurance that covers the cost of basic surgical procedures, such as minor surgeries and biopsies
A list of surgical procedures covered by an insurance policy and the maximum amount the policy will pay for each procedure
A method used by insurance companies to determine the appropriate amount to pay for medical expenses based on what is considered reasonable and customary in a given geographic area
A system used to determine the relative value of medical procedures, based on factors such as the time required to perform the procedure and the skill and training required
The maximum amount an insurance policy will pay for a particular medical procedure
A number used to calculate the reimbursement amount for a particular medical procedure, based on the relative value scale and other factors
Insurance that covers the cost of medical expenses incurred by the insured, specifically related to physician visits
Insurance that covers the cost of medical expenses incurred by the insured, specifically related to nursing care
Insurance that covers the cost of care in a convalescent or nursing home facility
Insurance that provides additional coverage on top of a basic major medical plan
A health insurance policy that covers only one person
A major medical plan that covers a wide range of medical expenses
A fixed amount that the insured must pay out of pocket before insurance coverage kicks in
A type of deductible that is only applicable to certain medical expenses
A deductible that applies to all medical expenses covered by an insurance policy
A deductible that applies to all members of a family covered by an insurance policy
A deductible that resets each calendar year
A deductible that applies to each separate medical condition or cause for treatment
A feature of some insurance policies that limits the amount of out-of-pocket expenses the insured is responsible for
Medical conditions that existed before the insurance policy was purchased
An employer-funded account that reimburses the insured for eligible medical expenses
Insurance policies that provide a fixed payment amount for each day of hospitalization
Insurance policies that limit the amount of coverage provided for specific risks or conditions
Insurance that provides a lump sum payment upon diagnosis of a serious illness such as cancer or heart disease
Insurance that provides coverage specifically for accidents, including medical expenses and disability benefits
Insurance that provides coverage for a limited period of time, typically up to 12 months
Insurance that covers the cost of hospitalization and includes a stop-loss feature, which limits the amount of out-of-pocket expenses the insured is responsible for
The 'time deductible' between onset of disability and the first benefit payment. Per this course: applies to SICKNESS only — accident claims pay immediately. Longer period = lower premium.
One-time waiting period at policy start during which sickness (never accidents) is not covered.
Totally disabled if unable to perform the duties of YOUR OWN occupation. Easier to qualify, more expensive.
Totally disabled only if unable to perform ANY occupation suited to your education, training, or experience.
Flat percentage (often 50%) of the total benefit when the insured can work but not full duties or full time.
Benefit proportional to lost income — a 40% earnings drop pays 40% of the total benefit.
Automatic total disability with no further proof: loss of sight, hearing, speech, or two limbs.

If the same disability returns within a stated period (often 6 months), it is a continuation — no new elimination period. But full recovery + return to work = a NEW disability: new elimination period, fresh benefit period.
Pays the business's rent, utilities, and employee salaries while the owner is disabled. Does NOT replace the owner's income.
Funds the purchase of a disabled partner's business interest; usually a lump sum after a long elimination period.

Premiums are waived while the insured is totally disabled, typically after 90 days, keeping the policy alive. Found in disability income policies (NOT medical expense); may apply retroactively; usually drops off at age 60–65.
Pays an extra benefit while the insured awaits or is denied Social Security disability; reduced if Social Security pays.
An insurance policy that provides regular payments to the insured person in case of a disability that prevents them from working and earning an income
The loss of income or earning capacity due to a disability or death, which can have a significant impact on the financial well-being of the individual or their dependents
The regular payments made to the insured person under a disability income insurance policy
An insurance policy that covers disabilities resulting from accidents or sicknesses that occur outside of the insured person’s occupation
An insurance policy that covers disabilities resulting from accidents or sicknesses that occur in the insured person’s occupation

The insured person is considered totally disabled if they are unable to perform the duties of their own occupation or any other occupation that they are reasonably qualified for, due to an injury or illness.
Refers to the ability of the insured person to perform any occupation that they are reasonably qualified for, rather than just their own occupation
Refers to the ability of the insured person to perform the duties of their own occupation, rather than any other occupation that they may be qualified for
A provision in an insurance policy that presumes the insured person to be disabled under certain circumstances, such as the loss of sight, hearing, or limbs
Assumes or considers something to be true, without requiring proof or evidence
A disability that does not completely prevent the insured person from working but still results in a loss of income or earning capacity
A fixed amount of benefit paid out under an insurance policy, regardless of the insured person’s income or earning capacity
A benefit paid out under an insurance policy that is calculated based on the insured person’s loss of income or earning capacity
A provision in an insurance policy that covers disabilities resulting from accidental bodily injuries
Sicknesses or medical conditions that the insured person had before taking out the insurance policy
A waiting period before benefits become payable under an insurance policy
The payment made to the insured person under an insurance policy in case of a covered event
Disabilities that recur after a period of recovery which may be covered under an insurance policy
Injuries that do not result in a disability but still require medical attention and treatment
A provision in an insurance policy that allows the insured person to choose between different benefit amounts and elimination periods
A rider that coordinates the benefits of an insurance policy with the benefits of the Social Security system
A rider that adjusts the benefit amount of an insurance policy based on changes in the cost of living
A rider that allows the insured person to purchase additional insurance coverage at predetermined intervals, without requiring medical underwriting

A period of time after an insurance policy is issued during which the insurer may cancel the policy if the insured person fails to meet certain requirements, such as paying the premiums or passing a medical examination
Pure accident insurance: pays a predetermined lump sum for accidental death or loss of limbs/sight. A VALUED contract — never pays for sickness.
The AD&D death benefit — the full stated amount paid for accidental death.
The AD&D dismemberment/loss-of-sight benefit — usually a percentage of the principal sum.
Pays 2x–4x the principal sum when accidental death occurs under specified circumstances (e.g., common-carrier accident).
Strict definition: BOTH the cause and the result must be unintentional. Jumped off the ladder? Not covered.
Broad definition: only the resulting injury must be unintentional. Jumped and got hurt? Covered. More favorable to the insured.
Covers death/dismemberment from one specific named risk only — e.g., an airport travel-accident policy.
Covers an unusual hazard standard policies won't — e.g., a violinist insuring her hands.
Accidental death must occur within 90 days of the accident (not 60). Dismemberment can include permanent LOSS OF USE — but losing fingers without the whole hand doesn't count.
Per this bank: principal sum pays for LOSS OF LIFE ONLY; ALL dismemberments (hands, partial foot, eye, fingers) fall under the capital sum schedule.
Hospital insurance: inpatient care, skilled nursing after a 3-day stay, hospice. Premium-free with 40 quarters of covered work.
Medical insurance: doctors and outpatient care. Monthly premium; pays 80% of approved charges after the deductible.
Medicare Advantage — private plans replacing original Medicare; requires enrollment in both Part A and Part B.
Prescription drug coverage through private plans; late-enrollment penalty without creditable coverage.
How Part A measures hospital use: starts at admission, ends 60 days after discharge; each has its own deductible.
Depleting assets to qualify for Medicaid; look-back rules penalize transfers made to qualify.
Compulsory hospital insurance financed by FICA payroll taxes; provided automatically when a qualified person applies for Social Security.
60 extra hospital days Part A provides after 90 days in a benefit period are exhausted — once used, gone forever.
Part A pays up to 100 days of skilled nursing care per benefit period after a qualifying hospital stay.
Private duty nursing, private rooms, first 3 pints of blood, SNF past 100 days, charges above approved amounts, care outside the U.S., custodial care, and well care (dental/vision/hearing/physicals).
Working 65+ employee at an employer with 20+ employees: the employer plan is PRIMARY, Medicare is SECONDARY.
Transferring assets below market value within the 5-year look-back delays Medicaid eligibility (Medicaid estate-planning penalty).
Gave states options to modernize Medicaid: cost sharing, flexible benefits, healthy opportunity accounts.
Created 1990 to expand access to health insurance for uninsured children.
Employers provide WC through a state program, a private insurer, or self-insurance. No-fault; employees contribute nothing.
Premium-FREE for those with 40 work quarters (others may buy in); everyone pays the Part A deductible per benefit period.
Standardized lettered Medicare supplement plans filling Medicare's deductibles and coinsurance; identical benefits per letter across insurers.
6-month guaranteed-issue window starting when a person is 65+ and enrolled in Part B; no underwriting.
Daily medical care by licensed professionals under a physician's direction.
Help with daily living (bathing, dressing, eating) by non-medical aides — the most common LTC need; NOT covered by Medicare.
Inability to perform 2 of 6 ADLs (bathing, dressing, eating, transferring, toileting, continence) or cognitive impairment.
Qualifying LTC policies let buyers protect assets equal to benefits paid when later applying for Medicaid.
NAIC standard core benefits: Part A coinsurance, first 3 pints of blood/year, 20% Part B coinsurance. Does NOT cover the Part A deductible.
Duplicate coverage — illegal to sell a Medicare Advantage plan to someone who owns a Medigap policy.
Assistance for people who lost full independence, typically needed beyond 90 days; triggered by inability to perform ADLs.
LTC insurance pays a set dollar amount for each day of specified care, regardless of actual expenses.
Acute = fully recoverable with care (pneumonia). Chronic = manageable, not curable (diabetes, asthma, heart disease).
Nursing care under physician supervision, in a facility or at home, provided periodically — NOT 24-hour continuous (that's skilled care).

term: Home health / adult day / respite care
definition: Home health = care in the insured's home. Adult day care = daytime center care. Respite = short-term relief for the family caregiver.
Benefit periods usually 2–6 years; issue-age limits ~80 (some to 89); elimination periods 0–365 days (longer = cheaper); must be guaranteed renewable.
Drug/alcohol dependence, self-inflicted injuries, acts of war, nonorganic mental illness. Alzheimer's and dementia ARE covered.
Age and health, type/level of benefits, presence and length of elimination period, and riders — NOT solely age/health/benefit type.
Group LTC: no individual underwriting, covers multiple family members. LTC benefits are excluded from income (tax-free).
State/industry partnerships controlling Medicaid costs: benefits paid by a partnership policy = assets disregarded for Medicaid qualification (no impoverishment).
The policy plus the attached application is the whole agreement; only an executive officer of the insurer — never the agent — may modify it.
After 2 years, the insurer cannot void the policy or deny claims for application misstatements, except fraudulent ones.
Time to pay an overdue premium with coverage still in force: 7 days (weekly premium), 10 days (monthly), 31 days (all other modes).
Restoring a lapsed policy. With a conditional receipt, automatic on the 45th day unless disapproved. Accidents covered immediately; sickness only after 10 days.
Insured must notify the insurer within 20 days of a loss, or as soon as reasonably possible.
Insurer must send claim forms within 15 days of notice; otherwise the insured may submit proof in any written form.
Written proof must be furnished within 90 days of the loss.
The insured must wait 60 days after proof of loss to sue and, in Florida, has up to 5 years to bring action.
Benefits are adjusted to what the premium would have bought at the true age — the policy is not voided.
10 days to return a health policy for a full refund; 30 days for Medicare supplement and long-term care policies. Starts at delivery.
Removes a named preexisting condition from coverage so an otherwise uninsurable applicant can get a policy.
Lets the insured buy additional coverage at set dates without new evidence of insurability.
The single group policy issued to the employer; covered employees receive certificates of insurance.
The document given to each group member as evidence of coverage under the master contract.
Federal law (employers with 20+ employees) letting covered persons continue group coverage at up to 102% of the premium after qualifying events.
18 months for termination/reduced hours; 29 months if disabled; 36 months for dependent events (death, divorce, Medicare entitlement, aging out).
§627.6692 — continuation rights (generally 18 months) for employees of small employers (under 20) not covered by federal COBRA.
31 days after leaving the group to convert to an individual policy without evidence of insurability, at higher individual rates.
Rules deciding which of two plans pays first so combined payments never exceed 100% of the claim.
For dependent children under both parents' plans, the plan of the parent whose birthday falls earlier in the calendar year is primary.
The waiting time a new employee must serve before becoming eligible for the group plan.
The window when eligible employees can join the group plan without evidence of insurability.
A principle of insurance where the insurer seeks to restore the insured to the same financial position as before the loss.
A contract where both parties must perform certain duties to make the contract enforceable.
A person in a position of trust, especially regarding financial matters like collecting premiums.
Offering something of value (like a portion of the commission) to an applicant to induce a sale.
An insurance company incorporated in the state where it is doing business.
An insurance company incorporated in another state.
An insurance company incorporated in another country.
A service provider that provides both the health care services and the financing for them.
A network of healthcare providers that offer services at a reduced rate to subscribers.
A type of health plan that allows members to choose between in-network and out-of-network care.
A federal program providing benefits to workers who become totally and permanently disabled.
The rate at which death occurs within a specific group.
A policy where the policyowner is eligible to receive dividends or share in the insurer's profits.
A policy where the policyowner does not share in the insurer's profits or receive dividends.
A practice where investors induce individuals to take out life insurance for the benefit of the investor.
The period after the premium due date during which the policy remains in force.
A period during which a new policyowner can review the policy and return it for a full refund.
A provision preventing the insurer from challenging the validity of a policy after it has been in force for a set period (usually 2 years).
The legal process by which an insurer seeks recovery from a third party responsible for a loss.
A federal law allowing employees to continue group health coverage after leaving a job.
Federal legislation aimed at increasing healthcare access and affordability.
A policy provision where the insurer must renew the policy as long as premiums are paid, but premiums can be increased by class.
A policy provision where the insurer cannot cancel the policy or increase premiums as long as they are paid.
A policy that the insurer can terminate at any time.
A contract that pays the actual amount of the loss up to the policy limit (e.g., medical expense insurance).
Headed by the elected CFO; regulates insurance agents, investigates agent misconduct, handles consumer complaints. Enforces the Insurance Code.
The elected official who heads DFS, oversees 15 divisions and offices, sits on the Financial Services Commission, and serves as the State Fire Marshal. NOT the Insurance Commissioner.

Headed by the Insurance Commissioner (appointed by the Financial Services Commission); regulates insurers, issues certificates of authority, approves policy forms and rates, conducts market conduct examinations.
Composed of the Governor, Attorney General, CFO, and Commissioner of Agriculture. Manages OIR and the Office of Financial Regulation. Appoints the Insurance Commissioner.
Appointed by the Financial Services Commission to head OIR. Monitors company filings and pricing. NOT the same person as the CFO.
The state license required for an insurer to transact insurance in Florida. Acting as an insurer without one is a third-degree felony.
Includes solicitation, initial discussions, effectuation of a contract, and any post-effectuation activity related to insurance.

Requires being 18+, a Florida resident, fingerprints/background check, completion of a 40-hour pre-licensing course, and passing the state exam. The license is perpetual unless suspended, revoked, or terminated for non-appointment.
An insurer's authorization for an agent to transact business on its behalf. Renews every 24 months. License terminates after 48 months (4 years) without one.
24 hours every 2 years (drops to 20 hours once licensed 6+ years), including a mandatory 4-hour Law & Ethics Update course.
Licensee must notify DFS in writing within 30 days of any change to name, home/business/mailing address, phone, or email. Fine: $250 first offense, $500 each subsequent offense.
An agent must notify DFS in writing within 30 days of pleading guilty to or being found guilty of a felony. Failure can result in license suspension or revocation.
An insurance agency requires a license. Exception: a single licensed agent operating in their own name without other licensees does not need a separate agency license.
Premiums collected are trust funds belonging to the insurer or insured — never the agent. Commingling or failing to remit is misappropriation (criminal).
Policy documents, summaries, and endorsements must be retained for at least 5 years following the policy's expiration.
No person other than a licensed and appointed agent may accept any commission or other valuable compensation for soliciting and negotiating insurance.

term: Florida Life and Health Insurance Guaranty Association (FLAHIGA)
definition: Protects policyholders from insurer insolvency. Covers annuity, health, and Medicare supplement contracts. Does NOT cover contracts issued by fraternal benefit societies.
An insurer experiencing financial difficulties but not yet insolvent.
An insurer unable to pay its claims and declared insolvent by a court.

Giving the impression a product is required or included when it isn't, or charging for coverage without the applicant's prior informed consent. Starting a policy without the property owner's knowledge.
When an agent obtains a license solely to write on themselves, family, officers, directors, partners, stockholders, or employees. Agent must write at least 75% other similar business.
Intentionally making false statements to convert an existing policy or obtain insurance from a DIFFERENT insurer through misrepresentation.
Inducing a client to purchase unnecessary policies with the SAME insurer to generate commissions.

Legal in FL under strict rules: rebate must come from agent's commission, be available to all in the same actuarial class, and the percentage cannot discriminate. Cannot be given at the agent's discretion.
Making false, malicious statements that harm the reputation of another person or insurer. Includes libel (written) and slander (spoken). Must be maliciously critical.
Boycott = refusing business to force compliance. Coercion = using force/threats. Intimidation = using fear/threats to manipulate. All are unfair trade practices.
Regulatory enforcement action. Violating one may result in a penalty of no more than $50,000 and/or suspension or revocation of license.
Gives the state the ability to fine, issue cease and desist orders, and impose penalties against insurers and producers for prohibited practices.
Illegal or unethical actions by insurers aimed at minimizing, denying, or delaying claims.
All advertisements must state the actual insurer's name. Contract invitations must include the form number. Each insurer must maintain an Advertising File.
Maximum value of permitted gifts to clients and prospects is $100.
If a license is revoked for improperly offering insurance to a person 65 or older, the department may not thereafter grant any insurance license to that individual — permanent revocation.
Providing false or misleading information to a client to induce them to purchase insurance. A lie or untruth is a misrepresentation.
Knowingly false statements on applications or claims. Insurance fraud is a felony in Florida.
Submitting a fake or fraudulently signed insurance application or policy-related document to an insurer is prohibited.
Knowingly applying unequal rates, dividends, or benefits to people with the same risk or actuarial class. No discrimination based on race, color, creed, marital status, sex, or national origin.
An individual or organization that has a close relationship with an insurer, such as an officer, director, or shareholder.
The process of removing an individual or organization from their affiliation with an insurer.
Legal consequences for violating insurance laws and regulations; may include fines, suspension or revocation of license, or criminal charges.
A licensed professional who represents policyholders in negotiating claims with their insurance companies.
An insurance adjuster licensed to handle claims for all types of insurance, including property, casualty, and health.
An insurance agent who is not affiliated with a particular insurance company and can sell policies from multiple insurers.
A type of broker specializing in viatical settlements — the sale of a life insurance policy by a terminally or chronically ill policyholder for a discounted cash amount.

This term refers to a health insurance plan that was in existence before the Affordable Care Act (ACA) was enacted on March 23, 2010. These plans are exempt from certain ACA requirements, but they must meet certain criteria to maintain grandfathered status.
An employee who is expected to work a minimum number of hours per week, typically 30 or more, and is eligible for certain benefits, such as health insurance, paid time off, and retirement benefits

An insurance policy that pays the minimum monthly payments on a borrower’s loan or credit account if the borrower becomes disabled and unable 
to work
An insurance policy that protects the lender in case the borrower defaults on their mortgage payments
People or entities who owe money to another person or entity

A health insurance plan offered by an employer or other organization that provides coverage to a group of individuals, such as employees and 
their families

A person who is eligible for benefits under a health insurance plan, such as an employee or their spouse or 
dependent child

A specific event, such as marriage, divorce, birth or adoption of a child, loss of a job, or a change in health insurance coverage, that allows a person to enroll or make changes to their health insurance plan outside of the regular open 
enrollment period
The age at which most people become eligible for Medicare, a federal health insurance program for people who are 65 or older, or who have certain disabilities
The practice of persuading a policyholder to surrender or cancel an existing insurance policy and replace it with a new one that may not be in their best interest
Aggressive or manipulative sales techniques used to persuade someone to buy a product or service
Advertising to potential customers who have not expressed interest in a product or service, such as through a phone call, email, or direct mail

term: Notice regarding replacement of Medicare supplement coverage
definition: A document that must be provided to a person who is considering replacing their existing Medicare supplement coverage with a new policy
A period of time, usually 10-30 days, during which a policyholder can review their new insurance policy and cancel it for a full refund if they are not satisfied
Medical conditions or illnesses that a person has before they enroll in a health insurance plan

A period of time after a premium payment is due during which a policyholder can still make the payment without 
losing coverage
The unintentional termination of an insurance policy due to a missed premium payment or other administrative error

An insurance policy that covers the costs of long-term care, such as nursing home care, in-home care, and assisted 
living facilities
A person who has a long-term medical condition that requires ongoing medical care or assistance with daily activities
A condition that affects a person’s ability to think, reason, remember, and process information, such as Alzheimer’s disease or dementia

Medical and personal care services that are necessary to help a person with a chronic illness or disability with their daily activities, such as bathing, dressing, 
and eating
A facility that provides care and supervision for adults during the day, typically for people who are elderly, disabled, or have cognitive impairments
A residential facility that provides assistance with daily activities, such
Medical and personal care services provided in a person’s home, such as nursing care, physical therapy, and occupational therapy
Residential facilities that provide around-the-clock medical and personal care services to people who are unable to care for themselves at home

Assistance with activities of daily living, such as bathing, dressing, and eating, provided to a person who is unable to care for themselves due to illness, injury, 
or disability
A period of time, typically 30-90 days, during which a new employee must wait before they are eligible for certain benefits, such as health insurance or retirement benefits
A person who is eligible for coverage under a health insurance plan due to their relationship to the primary policyholder, such as a spouse or child
An employee who is eligible for coverage under a group health insurance plan, typically due to working a certain number of hours per week or meeting other eligibility criteria
A requirement that insurance companies must offer coverage to all applicants who meet certain criteria, regardless of their health status or medical history
An employer with fewer than 50 full-time employees or their equivalent
An insurance company that offers health insurance plans to small employers

The ability to continue health insurance coverage when a person changes jobs or loses their job, typically through a continuation of coverage option such 
as COBRA
Private insurance policies that provide additional coverage to Medicare beneficiaries to help pay for out-of-pocket costs such as deductibles, copayments, and coinsurance
Services, medical equipment, and supplies furnished by a provider
Each member of the group pays a premium, a fixed sum per month, whether or not the person uses the services.
Delineate the arrangement between the subscriber or group of subscribers and the HMO
Assets of the HMO, if made immediately available, would not be enough to discharge. The HMO is unable to pay its debt as they occur in the ordinary course of business.
Any physician, hospital, organization, or other person or institution that furnishes healthcare services and is licensed or otherwise authorized to participate in Florida
A person or entity who has a health maintenance contract with the HMO, pays the fee, and uses the services of the HMO
A fixed amount paid by the HMO to a health care physician or provider in exchange for the medical services rendered by that provider

This refers to the specific dollar amount that the HMO subscriber must pay for certain covered health care services. These amounts are typically small and must be approved by the Office of Insurance Regulation.
An organization that delivers basic medical services at a specific location for the members of a group who make regular premium payments in exchange for those services
Making an oral or written statement or any pamphlet, circular, article, or literature that is false or malicious, critical of any person and that is calculated to injure such person

Knowingly making a false or fraudulent representation on, or relative to, an application for an HMO contract for the purpose of obtaining a fee, commission, money, or other benefit from any HMO agent or representative, broker, or individual

Knowingly making any misleading representations or incomplete or fraudulent comparisons of any HMO contract or HMO organization or any insurance policy or insurer for the purpose of inducing any person to lapse, forfeit, terminate, surrender, retain, or convert any insurance policy or HMO contract, or to purchase an insurance policy or HMO contract with another insurance company or HMO
Healthcare management stressing, preventative health care, early diagnosis, and treatment on an outpatient basis

CAP was established to protect the subscribers of HMOs against the failure of the HMO to perform its contractual obligations due to its insolvency. The law specifically prohibits the use of the CAP in any way in the sale of the HMO contract, even if it is for the purpose of reassuring the prospect.

The law requires that each HMO that offers a group plan within Florida must have an open enrollment period of no fewer than 30 days every 18 months. During that time, each eligible group member may enroll regardless of health history.

Every HMO contract must include a benefit extension clause that ensures that, in the event the HMO dissolves, payments will continue for any approved claims and benefits that were initiated while the contract was in effect.
